What does this mean?
Pretty simple: if you assign the value of a variable of type int to another variable of type int, both variables contain identical bits but they are completely independent: change the content of any of them, and another will be unaffected.
If you assign a variable containing a pointer (or consisting of a single pointer) to another one, they both, again, will contain identical bits and are independent in the sense that changing those bits in any of them will not affect the other.
But since the pointer in both these variables contains the address of the same memory location, using those pointers to modify the contents of the memory location they point at will modify the same memory.
In other words, the difference is that an int does not reference anything while a pointer naturally references another memory location鈥攂ecause it contains its address.
Hence, if a type contains at least a single pointer (it may do so by containing a field of another type which itself contains a pointer, and so on鈥攖o any nesting level), values of this type will have reference assignment semantics: if you assign a value to another variable, you end up with two values referencing the same memory location.

鹿 An interface value is a pair of pointers: to the variable containing the value you have put into the interface value and to a special data structure inside the Go runtime which describes the type of that variable.
So while you can put a slice value into a variable of type interface{} directly鈥攊n the sense that it's perfectly fine in the language鈥攊f the value's type is not itself a single pointer, the compiler will have to allocate on the heap a variable to contain a copy of your value there, and store a pointer to that new variable into the value of type interface{}.
This is needed to hold that "everything is always passed by value" semantics of the Go assignments.
Consequently, if you put a slice value into a variable of type interface{}, you will end up with a copy of that value on the heap.
Because of this, keeping pointers to slices in data structures such as sync.Map makes code uglier but results in lesser memory churn.

So if the object wraps some precious resource, that resource might get reclaimed way past the moment in time the last live reference to the enclosing object was lost, and it might even not get reclaimed at all鈥攁s has been well explained by @twotwotwo in their respective answer.
This means a GC thread which is about to collect a dead object might (and usually will) be not the thread(s) which executed that object's code when it was alive

In other words, it's much easier to control 鈥?and to reason about 鈥?object destruction when it is explicitly coded into the program's flow: both for specifying when the object has to be destroyed, and for guaranteeing proper ordering of its destruction with regard to destroying of the data structures external to it.
